Welcome to the second of five calls in the Clayton Make-Peace Make Money as a Copywriter Teleconference Series. My name is Bob Bly and we are Wendy Make-Peace. Hello there. And Clayton Make-Peace. Otherwise known as the Redhead. Otherwise known as the Redhead. This call is lengthened. We were scheduled to do an hour and we have lengthened it to 90 minutes. We did that because last week we thought we were really running behind, but it turns out we were really on track. I thought we were behind for call number one, which was the subject had to get every penny of your worth, but we actually covered everything. We talked about the reasons why all of us deserve the same royalties the big copywriters get. We talked about the way to become a power player in the client relationship, win-win compensation arrangements for every kind of work you do, how to make the client more profitable for you, how to make each client more profitable for you by writing more for them and other secrets. We talked about contracts in detail, including landmines to avoid in freelance copywriting contracts and how to collect every penny of the royalties, do you? The second call, which is today, the subject is how to keep your clients coming back to you over and over again forever. This call will be focusing on the care and feeding of your clients, customer service, client service, how to keep your clients coming back to you over and over again with big advanced checks in hand, ready to hire you to write more copy. The items we're going to cover on today's call are as follows. First what happens when you get a job for a client and your package doesn't work because sometimes they don't. Can you get hired again? I'm going to talk about that. What clients say to you behind your back and how to turn even the difficult clients into nice, friendly, easy to work with clients. Common blunders that get copywriters fired and how to avoid them, how to keep a client in the revision process from destroying your copy, the kind of critiques or crits you see and how to handle them, how to instantly end on a copywriting argument and keep your strongest copy intact, and times when you should demand your kill fee, kiss the client by and move on to greener pastures. This is what we're going to copy in call number two. We're really not behind, but we'll use the time. We're going to start off with those items. I'm going to ask Clayton to start and give us what's on that menu. We're going to go through it and cover everything. Then, in the extra time remaining, especially since we've budgeted an extra half hour, we're going to get to some of those great questions that you had emailed us last week that we were not yet able to get to. Clayton, does that sound like a fair way to proceed? That would be good to me. All right. Let me ask you the first one really intrigued me. I get this question all the time from beginning copywriters. Next to you, I probably get approached and ask questions more by beginning copywriters, I think more than anyone else who's around. The common one is, what happens? You keep saying when you have the control, the client will love you. What happens when I get hired to write a package and I write a package that either doesn't beat the control or worse. It's a bomb. I had a couple people said, I wrote a package and really got any orders. Then you're dead, right? Then you're destroyed at that client and they'll never hire you again. Isn't that right? Not really. If you're working with a company that has any experience in direct mail, especially, you're going to find that they're not that demanding. They're not going to try you once and then can you. Every good marketing director knows that there are a lot of reasons why even brilliant copy fails. They have a long experience working with people like you and me and Gary Bensevig and Jim Rutsch and they know that even A-level writers don't hit them out of the park on every try. If they like your copy, they feel that had a visceral appeal, it's very likely that they'll want to come back to you. Just off the top of my head, there are six or seven different reasons that have nothing to do with your talent as a writer or the power of your copy that could be behind a less than satisfactory result, especially in the publishing industry. Also in other industries that you'll write for. The main theme of your package, for example, is a key. You may decide, for example, if you're writing a financial piece, that the biggest news today is rising energy prices. You may focus the main headline, the opening, and the main theme of your package on rising energy prices. Between the time you write the copy and the time it gets in the mail, which can quite often be months, there may be another theme that comes up that investors are far more interested in, and perhaps a competitor or someone else has written on that. As a result, your copy doesn't become the new control. Even in writing for non-publishing clients, you may choose a particular benefit that resonates best with you and make that the main theme of your copy. You may find out by mailing that that theme doesn't resonate as well as your market, as a theme that somebody else has used. The main theme is a key. I've hired a lot of times to launch new products. The client wants to give the product the very best chance of making it, and so he goes to writers that have a good track record with him. Launches are low-percentage deals. They can be a grand slam or they can be a strikeout just as easily. Quite often, if it's a launch product, the first time it's been promoted, it may just be that the product itself doesn't resonate with the marketplace. I've had situations where we had an editor of a newsletter, for example, who was a little odd looking and did our launch as we normally would for his product and had his picture on the front of the backlog, and it didn't work very well. We went back and retested some headlines, and we retested a design without a photograph of the editor of the product. We got the control we were looking for. The product could be one of the things. Another is the offer. Whenever you're making an offer in one of these promotions, you quite often will have free gifts that you give away. The free gifts that you chose, the premiums that you chose to offer, don't have the same appeal to the market as the premiums another writer may have chosen. In some cases, you have no choice in that matter. Many times you're told which free gifts you're going to give away. Another problem that I run into quite often these days, especially, is compliance. Both in the health and the financial industries, which are the areas that I work in most, the level of compliance, or let me make that clearer, there are regulatory agencies that are, and the next issue of TTP is on this, by the way, and there's some good stuff in there for you. If you're a beginning writer and you want to understand legal issues, it's our scary Halloween issue. In all industries, the Federal Trade Commission regulates what can be said and what can't be said in sales copy. Then in individual industries, there are also other regulatory bodies that rule on what you can say and what you can't say. In the financial area, it's the Securities and Exchange Commission, the Commodities Futures Commission, and also the National Association of Security Steelers. In the health area, it's the Food and Drug Administration. Each client implements those regulations at a different level and finds different regulations within those bodies of regulations to be more important than others. If there has been a change in how the client enforces compliance on its copy, or if you've chosen a theme that requires stricter compliance measures than one your competitor has chosen, you could wind up with weaker copy and therefore not win. I just had a situation that was legally related and had to do with regulatory compliance, but I wrote what I thought was kick butt copy for package on the dangers of prescription drugs. Everybody was very excited about the copy, and it led right off with the Vioxx scandal and the fact that as many as 140 million people, 140,000 people have had heart attacks after taking Vioxx for pain relief. The attorneys killed all mention of any drug in the package. You actually can't use the name Vioxx? In this case, I couldn't because the client was concerned about liability with the... With Merck. Actually, I nailed Pfizer too because I had Vioxx and Vioxxstrawl in there. The client's own legal parameters can be a limitation, and they can be more of a limitation on your package if you choose a theme that flirts with those limitations than on the control or a package somebody else writes. Now I have to stop and ask you. The immediate thought that comes into our minds is, you are Clayton Macfies. Did you just say to the client, look. Are you at risk? Are you comply or did you say, hey, you're making a dumb mistake? Did you argue at all? When you're not talking to the lawyer, arguing is fruitless. It's pointless because the lawyer is basically saying, look, the copywriter is probably right about this. But the fact is, if you mail this, we can find ourselves in a lawsuit that will cost us $100,000. At that point, unless your best friends are the president and company, compliance with both liability measures and also with regulatory measures, that is a business decision. There's no such thing as a totally sanitized sales message. Any regulator can nail you at any time for a reason. Any consumer can sue you at any time for any reason. So it's not a black and white thing. Each business owner has to make his own call on where a long line of compliance or legal love, hiding under the bed, he's going to do. They've established those parameters long before you came to the scene. Usually arguing that is worthless. However, you have two choices at that point. You can go into the package and you can say, look, if I can't name these drugs, that was the theme of the whole package. I'm going to get killed in the mail if I stick with this theme, but can't name the drugs. Therefore, let me rewrite the front end of the package with different themes. Or you can say, as I did in this case, Vioxx is enough in the news right now that I can go ahead with this package and say, the aridist drugs that they gave you, put tripled your risk of a heart attack and go that way. In that case, that's what I did. So a package might not work because they're too compliant. They're interpreting the compliance regulations very strictly and following them. Or the offer doesn't work like the premiums or the product in the launch is a new product and the market doesn't want that product. Or you picked the wrong theme, let's say for a financial package, you picked inflation and oil does better. That's four reasons why a package wouldn't work. What are the others you had? For new mailers, the list selects, the media he uses, the people he sends the sales message to, whether it's direct mail or print or internet or wherever, he may not have accurately targeted his market. I'm in a situation right now where I have a package that I just created for a health company and I hired Carleen to write the package. And she came back, Carleen is Afro-American, she came back and read after reading the research and gave me a package that said basically the darker your skin, the more at risk you are for heart disease because that really resonated with her and she felt that we could get a 5% response from anybody on the, from any people on our file. I on the other hand felt like I wanted a broader appeal. So I took her copy and we're going to mail it but we're going to test it against another version where we focus on heart disease up front, something that everybody's concerned about. Now when we test those two packages, I went to the client and I said on Carleen's cell and also for a small part of my cell, I want you to be sure to include some of that list. Crodell has some great lists on health for Afro-Americans for example and I suggested that you include a component of that in both panels so we can actually measure what kind of list Carleen gets when she's talking to ethnic minorities and I think that's a key there, the list selects or the media you're going to need to be right for the message. Another thing that has nailed me time after time again is just timing. If you're in the mail with a package about how inflation is as I was back in 1979, if you're in the mail with a package that talks about how inflation is going to go through the roof and every dollar you have is only going to be worth a penny and all of that kind of thing and then Ronald Reagan gets elected president and you go into the mail, you're going to get killed because your constituents, the people that you're talking to feel like they've just solved the inflation problem. We saw 9-11 kill direct mail response at the beginning of the wars in Iraq and Kuwait killed response, the OJ Simpson trial killed response, people were watching their televisions instead of reading the mail and so any world event of any consequence can kill response to a mail package. Then finally there's, you don't know what else is going to be in the mail box or what other people are going to be reading when your package arrives and so if a competitor, another company is mailing a package on the same theme or another company has come up with another theme or another headline concept that's extremely strong and your package and their package are on the same day, you may find that your response is less than you'd hope. These are just, that's just seven things very quickly that major mailers know affect response and so another one might be the graphic design of the piece. They say that people who love sausage or direct mail should never see either one being created. The direct mail stuff that we do whether it's copy or design is a messy process, it's a creative process and it's very hard to get a consistent product out of graphic designers especially and I've seen situations where changing the design of a package increased response 20 to 30 percent. You're not the only one here involved in this, the list broker, the manager of the media plan, the graphic designer, the product, the guy who designed the product and before the editor, all these people have input into the success or failure of a promotion. You are saying you believe that a sophisticated client knows this and therefore if they liked your package but it doesn't work and they feel that one of these factors could have made that so they will try you again? All of the things being equal, yes. So long as the relationship was good, so long as you were responsive, some of the things we'll get to in a minute, so long as you handled the client a professional way, so long as they felt your copy was strong, you're not going to be axed just for that. Well let's talk about that because you often hear some people say if you can write a winner, the world is your oyster and nothing else matters. If you write killers, you've said or you're hinting that you can write winners but if there's other problems like you're a great copywriter but you're kind of a jackass to people, they're not as eager to hire you. Does it matter how the customer service aspect of it or is it just what you write? No, no. Everything I've said about these people, their lives rising and falling on finding good writers is absolutely true. However, there is a line that they won't go over. I could name one guy right now and there are others who's an excellent writer and has kicked out control after control for 30 years now. Nobody wants to hire him because he's so hard to work with. One of the things I think you'll find is as you meet top copywriters is when you meet top freelancers, they're invariably nice people. If you've ever talked to Gary Bencevanga, he's unassuming, nice, wants to help you, just one of the kindest people you'll ever meet. Same as you with Jim Rottz. I like to think that I'm fairly open and helpful with the information and advice I give. The ones who have risen to the top of the field are not egotistical, my way or the highway kind of people. They're the kind of people that will work with you and be a very helpful part of the team rather than somebody who says, look, you either fail this or forget it. Now, the guy I was thinking about, his copy is legendary, but he hasn't been able to build a freelance copywriting business of any consequence because he's so hard to work with. As a result, he's making millions developing and selling his own products, so no harm, no foul as far as he's concerned, but the clients that all of us are looking for just will not work with him. If you're difficult with the client, is it a multiple effect? Will they keep it to themselves? Do these guys talk to each other? They talk to each other all the time. In our business, especially Bob, as you know, it's very incestuous. They have something called the Publishers Roundtable. They actually go to some city in Europe or the United States once a year and hang out together for three or four days and share notes on copywriters. The marketing managers call each other up on the phone or now, I'm about lists that are working and compare notes on copywriters. People are talking all the time and because copywriters are a key part of this equation, we tend to be a topic of conversation. Again, this isn't just in the health and financial publishing area or just in the vitamin cells area. You get involved in anything else, any other niche, whether it be catalogs or even a niche in a niche, a particular kind of catalog marketing or anything else that you're doing. There is an industry, there is an association and these people talk to each other. Plus, if they're smart, they're selling each other's products too. Word will get around. If I'm working with a finite group of clients and they're talking about me behind my back, what are the things I can do to make them, when they talk about me, say nice things other than write, win, and copy? This is one of the things that we promised to discuss on this call. I'd say the first thing is, it's essential that you be prepared. The minute that you get a signed contract, the next thing that's going to happen is that the client is going to want to have a conference call with you or perhaps in some cases a live meeting with you and with other key people on their team. It could be the person who invented the product, it could be the editor of the newsletter, it could be the formula of the vitamin, and then also the marketing people who've been involved with the product. These people can be solid gold for you if you know how to listen to what they say because they've been involved or at least have access to hundreds of tests over the years where they've tested one kind of headline against another, one kind of design against another, one kind of theme against another. These people are going to be solid gold to you if you know how to listen to them. A lot of them will not say, we tested X, Y, Z back in such and such a year and these are the results but what they'll say is something much vaguer but they're trying to tell you, look, we tried this, we know how well it works. Those people are key. Anyway, so after you sign the contract, you're going to have this meeting. It's very important that you be prepared for that meeting, that you are very familiar with the product, that you're very familiar with the promotions that they've used in the recent past and that you have some grasp or handle on what their competitors are using right now to promote their products. So in preparation for that meeting, I usually ask for in the newsletter area a year of back issues, copies of the free gifts that they give away with the sale of the newsletter. And I ask for a biography on the editor if it's a three-dimensional product or a supplement. I ask for research on the supplement so I can take a look at what it contains, what it does and how competitors are selling a similar supplement. There are two full benefits to that. The first part of the benefit is you're going to know what you're talking about when you have that launch meeting. The second one is that the people who you're working with will be impressed with your professionalism. They'll be put at ease. They'll say, wow, this guy seems to really know what he's doing. So I think that's very important. Following along the same line, the second thing to do is at the meeting as you're brainstorming the question will always come up, what do you need to write this package? I think it's really important to know what you're going to ask for because, again, that reflects on your professionalism. I always ask for other promo copy they've used, both successful and unsuccessful. And for them to tell me which is which, I can usually guess, but not always. So I want to know that. And it's a surprise that can really give you a leg up when you see something and say, wow, this really stinks. And then you find out that was a big winner for them. Then that's a clue. That's a clue right there that you need to go farther than your own gut instinct on the product. Let me ask you something that I'm asked all the time. Let's say you're doing this and the client gives you a bunch of promotions for this product. And you notice that all the winners are kind of promotional looking jumbos, 9 by 12 envelopes. All the losers are number 10 letter packages, Magalongs, Tabloids, they all lost. Would you be smarter to, again, try something different than all their winning packages which are all the same, they're all jumbos? Or would you be better staying in that format? That's a great question. That's something that comes up at the beginning of this process every single time. I used to struggle with it tremendously. And what do I do here? Do I do a 6 by 9? Do I do a number 10? What do I do? Do a tabloid or a med-loid? My philosophy now is go with what's working on your first doubting. If the winner is a tabloid, then go with a tabloid. What you're counting on is that the power of your copy is going to be stronger than the copy of the power of the other guy's copy. And so if you introduce another variable into the mix, if he's doing an 8 and a half by 11 special report style format piece, it's 24 pages long, and you write a 24 page tabloid, well you've just introduced a new variable that's two-year detriment because that tabloid costs more to mail. So that means you have to get a higher response to break even, and that puts you at a disadvantage. So I tend to emulate what's working now. And as soon as I get a control on a product, I immediately look at the format and see if there are ways to save money on it. I just had this experience with Ford Room. I wrote a promotion for one of their books. Arthur Johnson, who's a great writer, has their control. Arthur's control was a tabloid-sized 16-pager. Mine was a copy that was challenging his control. It was a 24-page package. I pulled a higher response rate and a higher closing rate than Arthur did. But my piece cost more. So his remained the control. So then I had to go back and pair my copy down so that I could get my cost down to where Arthur's was. Now at that level, so long as I haven't heard my response rate and I don't think I have, I'll probably knock off Arthur's control. So I didn't learn the lesson on that. I learned the lesson and not been so cocky. I would have looked at Arthur's thing and I would have said, look man, this guy's got a 16-page tabloid-sized control. That's what I'm going to write. And I'm just going to make sure that I do everything I can possibly do to make my copy stronger than his. I think that's a much smarter choice. And I'm famous for going back then after the control was established and slicing and dicing a copy in different ways to turn it into a book-a-log, a six-by-nine, another format. So I'm taking my risk with something that's already proven. But to do this then, you've got to know what to ask the client for. In other words, you should ask them for what they would expect any competent, qualified writer would ask for. And the two basic things to ask for is the actual product, supplements, newsletters or whatever. And the promotion is the control and test, right? What else would you ask for? I also ask for, and they expect this. I think it's a blunder when you don't, raw testimonials. Most mailers, again, if you're starting with somebody who isn't really hip on mailing and stuff and internet promotion, they may or may not have testimonials to work with. Most major mailers do, most people who have been in the mail anytime do have testimonials. I always ask for raw testimonials. That's what the customer said, word for word. Warts and all. It can be grammatically incorrect, it can have horrible misspellings in it, it can rel on for pages, I don't care, I want to see it all. Because I don't want to trust somebody else to have edited that testimonial and to have really drawn the meat out of it. The Federal Trade Commission allows you to edit testimonials so long as you don't change their meaning. That gives you a lot of latitude in carving these testimonials down in a way that will really have great impact. So I don't trust that to other people. Another thing is public relations output. A lot of these companies use PR agents to get press releases out there and get mentions in the media. There's nothing more credible, there's no more better credibility boost than the fact that your client has been featured in the Wall Street Journal or major health publications, Prevention Magazine or someplace else. Or that people call him or call them on a regular basis for insights on the news or things like that. So you've seen these little as seen on TV stickers that they put all over everything that's been promoted on television. What they're saying there is that, look, this is a large credible company, we have national coverage and so you can believe what we tell you about this product. So PR output is very important. In Martin Weiss's things, I broke down the PR output into three major sidebars that I ran in his promotion. The first one was just pictures of him on Nightline and on CNBC and on Fox News talking with Neil Cavuto or Ted Koppel or these other people and talked about how the fact, just with a short blurb that says the year, 1989, Martin Weiss goes on Nightline to warn people about the insurance crisis. And then I had like eight or nine of those in a box, so it was media appearances. The next one was actual quotes from the media about Weiss that these were just little short one-liners. Four says this, Fortune says this and Barron says that and so forth. Another sidebar, I was fortunate enough to have a client who had been called to testify before Congress about a problem in the insurance industry and so I had pictures of him on Capitol Hill testing before a bunch of Congress people. All of these things were tremendous credibility builders in my packages and then I also ran sidebars showing one or two paragraphs of text from either an article that Martin had contributed to publication or that some publication had said about Martin. So PR output is important. If you have it, use it. We talked about the most successful promotions. Competitors are fielding for a similar product but also look outside of that a little bit. So for any direct mail that's come in in the last three or four months that there are people that have collected that they've pressed with, stuff that they think that there might be some application advice. They have enormous files of this kind of material and many other mayors do. Then articles, books or other things that have been authored by the client. In my area of health and finance, the editors of our newsletters are quite often but best selling authors. I've done packages for Barry Sears of the Zone Diet, Deepak Chopra and all of these guys and I required the client to send me everything that they had written so that I could read it and really familiarize myself with the tone of the author's voice as well as his message. Then finally the complete research hit on whatever theme you've chosen is your lead theme for the package. Some clients are better at this than others but almost every publishing client at least has editorial people who do the research for the regular issues of the newsletter like the premiums. So I ask for those people to help me. Let's say for example I'm doing a promotion on the emergence of China and India as the world's greatest economic hours. I don't want to spend hours and hours online looking up that stuff and reading it off. If they have a researcher there, chances are he's already done the research for articles or premiums that have been done and would be willing to do more and knows right where to go. The ability to do the Lexus Nexus searches and they're just good to go. It'll save you a lot of time on each job and in the early going will also enhance the perception and professionalism that the client has of you. So to make the clients say nice things when they're talking about me, I want to be prepared. I want to ask them for not only for what they expect me to ask them, if I don't feel that I don't know the way this is done. Well, yeah, it's going to help you and that you'll have more material to start with. So your first draft will be more on target but it's also going to make them put them at ease and feel that, wow, I'm working with a pro here, he knows exactly what he needs from me. Makes sense. What else can I do to make a good impression with my new client? If they don't offer being with the designer or the product or the author, then I would request it. I would say, you know, I want to dive into this. You've given me a lot of great stuff but I really want to pick this guy's brain. I want to get a sense of his passion and get to know his personality a little bit. Clients have flown their main guys in to see me. Other times I have to go see them. But it's a very helpful time and I always ask the question, why are you doing this? Because I'm looking for passion and I'm looking for a commitment between the editor and the prospect. You know, the old, I'm not going to let you fail kind of thing. I got in this because it breaks my heart to see people dying unnecessary of heart disease. Or I got into this because I just saw too many people get waxed in the tech rack and lose their life savings. You know, and I vowed right then and there I would never let that happen again if I could do, if there was anything I could possibly do to stop it. And so I'm looking for passion and personality in that meeting. And also for a sense of the possible theme ideas going forward that the editor is most interested in, or in the case of a supplement product development in the industry that he's most excited about, that the formulator is most excited about. So request that meeting and have a half a dozen starter questions available to kind of work through all of that with the designer, the product or the editor. So request a meeting with the product developer or designer. Great. What else can I do to do a good job with the client but also show them that I'm a real professional? Right. Two more things that I do. I like to provide a platform. Now other people hate doing this. Carling Cole won't do it. She says, you know, it takes me as long to write the whole package as it does to write the platform. So she says, you know, pay me double and I'll give you a platform. Otherwise I'll just write the copy. Paris on the other hand will write the first, Paris Line Propolis will write the first several pages of the package and send it through just to get an early take on the tone and theme from the client. I like to, after having the meeting with the group and with the editor or the formator or whatever, I like to sit down and put together just flow of consciousness stuff on what I'm thinking about the product and the client and what I'm thinking about the, what shape the promotion piece will ultimately take. I send that through to the client and say, here are my preliminary thoughts. I'd like you to react to each one of them and let me know how you feel. So in your case, you could either create a platform, which is a proposal or a description of the process you have in mind to write or as in Paris' case, he gives them the actual copy, he gives them two or three pages of the copy. Extremely rough flow of consciousness. Here's kind of where I want to go. Here's what I want to do. What do you think? And it's their responses to that that's going to give you some clues. It'll save you a lot of time on second and third draft. Now your next comment about what to do to wow a client is something that I, being not a people person, have really done horribly. But you say communicate with the client at unexpected times. Why aren't these people busy? Yeah, they are, but the whole time they're busy. They've got this little thing in the back of their mind. Gee, I wonder how that copy's going. And they don't want to bother you because they know you're busy. They don't want to interrupt your workflow. They don't want you to feel pressured because they want quality. And they know if you're thinking about a deadline, they're going to get the copy on deadline, but it'll probably stick. If you're thinking about the copy, whether it comes in on deadline or not, it's going to be strong. So they don't want you to feel rushed or pushed. At least a smart client doesn't. So just dropping an email to them, hey, it's going great, or hey, I found this new webpage with some great new information on it. I have some new headlines that I just think are fantastic. It's going great. Confirm when you think that you'll have the copy on the first draft copy. Or just call them up on the phone and say, hey, how's it going? I thought I'd touch bases. I'm really having a lot of fun with this subject. It's coming along. And I think it's going to be gangbusters. Just check in with them from time to time. When you do that, they're going to, first of all, think about it from their position. They hire you. They give you thousands of dollars in an advanced fee. They clear an opening for you and they're all important mail schedule that will be vacant if you don't deliver on time. And then they're supposed to sit there in cooler heels for three weeks, four weeks, five weeks, six weeks while you're supposedly working on their copy. And they have no input from you whatsoever. Calling in even with questions or certainly any of the things that I've just mentioned goes a long way towards putting them at ease and feeling, wow, this is a comfortable professional relationship. So another tip for establishing yourself as a good professional, someone they could be happy they hire, is client contact. I think so. And don't be afraid to ask questions. A lot of people, you know, if you go in there under this guy that I am the world's greatest copywriter and I'm going to save your company and I'm going to write this big control and I know everything there is to know and you go in there like that, first of all, you've got a credibility problem because if they've been around for a long time, they know there is no such thing as world's greatest copywriter. The second thing that you've got going for you against you is that they're going to feel uncomfortable making suggestions or sharing ideas they've had with you. If instead you approach this as being a member of a team that you're excited to be on and you talk to them and you pick their brains and you find the people who are on the team, maybe the assistant marketing people or even down lower on the ladder and you just call them up and you ask them a couple of questions. And you've got to remember these are corporate animals, they all have managers, they all have meetings once a week where they're supposed to be able to tell their bosses how the copy is coming and they don't have a clue. Now here's what people are nervous about. What happens if I think I'm doing a good job but what happens if the client doesn't hire me back or they fire me? What are the common reasons why clients get so unhappy that they will stop doing business with a copywriter or even kick them off a job? I think the first one is really chemistry. And it's that whole thing. If you try to make it, I say quite often, first you make a friend then you make a sale. Well it's the same and that's true with copy but it's also true with dealing with clients. The chemistry is important and doing little things in order to allow the client internally to feel that you value them, you value their experience and that you're willing to listen to their ideas I think is very important. Another one is over-promising and under-performing. If you think it's going to take you three weeks to get the copy done, tell them four and deliver in three. I'm not saying a lot to them, I'm just saying I tend to be a beat-aloptimist. Wendy's always talking about how I describe something that needs to be done. Say it's easy, just do this, that and the other thing. Well it's not easy, it's never easy. It always takes longer than I think it will. So allow for that so that when you can deliver, you're delivering something within the timeframe if at all possible. Quality of the copy is more important than the deadline to me though. If it's not there on the deadline then I communicate and I'll say it's not quite there, I need another week or whatever and I try to keep the client up to date. But again that attitude is very important that I'm here to help you and I want to accommodate you. A lot of times people internally will actually sandbag or sabotage you, they feel that you're minimizing their importance to the process. So there are those people out there and the best way to neutralize them is to bring them into the process and make them to value. Another is failure to listen. And I think this comes back to the fearlessness that goes along with feeling freed asked questions. I can't tell you any times I brought in younger copy writers to work with a client and the client and I both tell him what's important to the client, what are the themes that have worked in the past, we've passed all of these things and this worked better than that and so forth. But then the copy comes back and it's like we never had that conversation. So I think it's really important to pay attention to the things that they're telling you and you can read between the lines in an attempt to deliver something that will be very close on first draft. The chemistry thing, I want to go back to that for a second, that's just really not something where you can go away and you can say well that person's just a jerk. If you weren't able to salvage a client with your own native charm then it's not that person's fault. There are people everywhere that are not going to get along with you. Maybe you look like an aunt that once spanked them or an uncle that got them in trouble once or something. You can't help that. But as a copy writer, as somebody who provides service to a company, it's your job to overcome that and to identify people that they're dealing with who are difficult and to find ways to win them over. Well, how could you win over someone who is difficult, let's say cold to you, you know, they answer you in monosyllables and you can tell they don't like you? Well sometimes it's not possible but I think making the effort is important and many times I found out that that person I thought who didn't like me was just intimidated or that person who didn't like me was new and felt like and just needed someone to say, hey look, I think you're great. I think you're doing a great job. You give me a lot of ideas here and copy their boss on the email when you say that. Just understand that people skills are important. They're important process here and that any relationship that's strained where other members of the team aren't really contributing to you and helping you weakens your overall effort. So look for the chemistry, be aware of chemistry and try to fix it if you can. Make sure you listen and don't over promise and then under deliver. What other big blunders do copy owners make get them the boot? I think one of the things that I run into with young writers all the time is that when the copy comes in they're apologizing for it and they say, well I didn't quite get a section writer. I don't like this argument or I don't really believe that. A lot of copy odds and beware. When I present my copy I try to make it a gal event. I don't oversell it. I don't say here's the best package that you'll ever be. But I let them know that I'm proud of the copy, that I'm proud of the effort and I have high hopes for it. I also make sure that when they look at my copy it looks like it was prepared with care. It's not all different sizes of type and colors and things all over the page. Clients tend to see that kind of thing and think, this guy's covering up for the fact that he thinks the copy's weak. He's got to go through and he's got to make this paragraph bigger than that paragraph and he's got to do all of these other crazy things. Almost trying to be a graphic designer in presenting a copy. I'll run my main head in 72 point type if I want to in my first half. Then my running text is all 12 point times. My subheads are all 14 point impact. My sidebars are all 12 point aerial or helvedic or veranda. With the subhead, with the headlines of the sidebars, 20 point times bold. And that's it. What type is the 72 point headlining? Say what? What type is the main headline? I tend to go with impact because it's a nice big fat sans-serif condensed face. Because it's condensed I can get more letters on a line with it which gives you a bigger headline size. My rule of thumb is if my headline is 36 point and I'm looking for no more than three lines for a headline. So if it's 36 point that's an okay headline. If it's 48 point that's a pretty good headline. If I can do it in 72 point that means now I've just done a two word headline. Maybe six letters. Right. I mean that's the best there is. That's like firing off a gun behind somebody. Yeah I just looked at impact. It's cool. I've noticed that you do that. I've been using aerial black but it's impacts a very nice headline type. So you want to make it look good. What about typos? Is that acceptable? Does it matter? Well you know you just all I worry about on my draft they're not expecting us to be copy editors or proof readers. I tell the story that one time when I delivered copy I had an email or actually it was a letter that I sent along with it and I said that this has not been pre-fruited yet. I misspelled the word proof right. They thought you were being funny. Yeah. So they thought I was being hilarious. So I've used it ever since but it was a mistake the first time. I just run a good spell checker on it. I'm not. I turn off the thing in word that checks your grammar. That thing just takes me off. But I just do a good spell check and then I go through and make sure that it reads well. I don't care about the 8th. What is it? It's just a copy writer AP rule. Sometimes I'll use the number 3 as a big ol' number and sometimes I'll spell it. So as long as it looks good it's professional. It looks like it's professionally presented, prepared and you don't apologize for it. Handing something that you think is good. Yeah. I've had copy writers where I wanted a 24 page piece from them and that requires 32 pages, single space roughly. Using the parameters I just outlined, 12 point running text and so forth. It'll be about 32 pages long in order to fill a 24 page mag log. So these guys send me a copy but all the running text is 16 points. Well that just insults my intelligence. They have written way too short and they're trying to cover up for it by running everything in large type. So don't insult their intelligence. Make sure that your finished product is professionally presented. Any of my mistakes I want to avoid making? I like to keep some copy writers will send a copy in with two or three different leads right up front. And I think that presents a client with a decision to make too early in the process. What I do is I like to pick my strongest, what I think is my strongest lead and put it up front so that the client can read my strongest headline deck copy, my opening copy and then all the way through my running text without interruption. Then after the last line of my sales letter or running text in the case of a mag log, I'll have a section that says sidebars. And in that section then I will have each sidebar that's going to punctuate that running text with an approximation of where it goes. This goes on page two, this is the bio. This goes with the running text on such and such and forth all the way through. And then finally I have my response device and back cover in the case of a self-mailing. And so they can go all the way through in sequence and really gauge the copy. Then if I have other leads or other headlines, then I add them to the very tail end of the document. And I do that even though I may not in love with them. They may be alternates, they may be things I'd like to test or they may just trigger ideas for discussion as we're going over the second draft later, going after that draft later. Now it's five of one but folks today we're not ending at one ending, we have until one thirty and we let you know that. So we have plenty of time and we're not rushed and don't feel panicked, you can just sit back and relax, take another sip of the coffee, we have at least another half hour with Clayton. So let me move on to the next topic, how to keep a client from killing your copy. Four types of critiques you'll see most often in how to handle it. And this is something that I hear from people all the time. They say, I'll get freelancers who will email me. I handed in this copy to the client and they say they hate it and the freelancer feels they're dead in the water. They don't know what to do, they're emotionally upset, the whole thing's falling apart. Yeah, well in copywriting as in life your emotions are probably your worst enemy. The first thing that a young copywriter has to get used to is putting yourself out there and having the response to your copy being less than you'd hoped. Some of the writers I work with are absolutely crushed emotionally by that and they get over it, you know, but it takes courage, it takes guts to pour your life into a piece of copy for a period of time, send it off to a bunch of near strangers and basically lay yourself there and say go ahead, you know, cut my heart out, you know, do whatever you want to. Tell me it's thanks if you want to. I mean that takes courage to make you all that vulnerable to somebody. What takes real fortitude is when the response comes back and it's not what you'd hoped. And the, I don't remember the last, well yes I do, I do remember the last time somebody said we don't like it. It was just a couple of weeks ago and it was Chris Morett. I had written a promotion piece for one of his financial editors and I had met with financial editor and talked with him and none of the people from Phillips in that meeting and the financial editor basically did a brain dump for me. I then took that and turned it into a platform that I was discussing earlier and sent it to the group at Phillips and they loved it. They had a couple of very minor comments on it. I then created the package along the lines of that platform and I got the call back that said we don't like it. And my response to that was simply well what don't you like? And they said well we think that your approach here is a little too general. Okay, we'd like to have something that's more specific. You're talking about how Wall Street rips off investors and we'd like to have something that's more topical. Okay, all right fine. It would have been nice to have heard that earlier but since I hadn't I just dove back into the copy and I created the second draft and they bought it just with no changes. So it's a process. Get used to the idea that it is a process and that it can be messy. So what are the four types of critiques that you see most often? Okay, well the one that you just mentioned almost never happens. It almost never happens if they say well we just don't like it. So you'll get four kinds of critiques. The first one is where the client identifies a problem that does not exist in the text and then suggests a solution that would actually hurt your response. If a client or a marketing manager is particularly detail oriented they may come with this kind of critique and if the problem doesn't exist like for example they'll say well you know I think people who will read this might think that. Well let's face it, people who read this are going to think a lot of things and quite often that kind of a crit is just you know somebody wanting to have something to say. It's not really even a crit. But when you identify something where the client says there's a problem you don't see the problem you know and he's suggesting a solution that's going to hurt then you just need to get him on the phone at that point and let me back up just a second if I might. I think it's important when you begin the process to set the ground rules for critting. In fact I do it even before I accept the assignment in the first place. And my ground rules are that if you when you have critiques or suggestions for my copy that's what they are. They're suggestions. They're not changes. Okay. They're suggestions. And you're hiring me to write this and so then I will make the decision as to whether your suggestion is one that warrants inclusion in my copy or not. If we reach a point where we can't agree on that then there needs to be somebody we can appeal to who will make the ultimate decision and it needs of course to be the person who's going to ultimately put his money behind this. But in terms of marketing managers and other people that you work with on a daily basis they're hiring you because you're the expert. If they were the expert they could write it themselves. And so other than legal issues, ethical issues or issues of fact I make sure that everyone understands that I get to make the other decisions. They can make decisions about fact, legal issues and ethical issues. But other than that I'm the copywriter. So that having prepared the way that way then when I get crits in that I think are going to hurt. It's very easy to pick up the phone and say hey look, you know, I don't think this is a problem and I just don't want to make a change here and here's why it isn't a problem and have the conversation with them. Nine times out of ten they'll let you win. The second kind of crit is when the client identifies a problem that doesn't exist but his solution be a strong addition to the copy. The love of it, you know, for what, because any idea no matter how it's derived is something that's going to put money in my pocket. So I tend to just congratulate them for being brilliant and just go straight to work figuring a way to really make that work to leverage the power of the new idea. The third way is when the client identifies a problem that does exist but his solution stinks. On those I just say I validate their observation. I say you know what, you're right, that's a problem, that's something I want to change. But let me think about how I want to do that. And I'll always say okay sure fine. And then you can go back in and you can implement a solution that is going to actually strengthen the copy. And then the fourth kind of crit, the one that you really want is the one where the client finds a problem in the copy or an opportunity in the copy and his suggested solution is brilliant. When that happens he's just made you money, you know, go ahead jump on a plane fly up there and kiss him on the cheek, he's a great guy. Those are the kinds of crit that you pray for. It's important to be able to personally separate your own emotions or feelings about the copy from your objective assessment of what will work and what won't work. And it's enough especially for young writers to figure out what you think is going to work. It's very easy to become confused when a client or somebody else jumps into the mix and starts giving you suggestions and ideas and crit. It's important to keep your own sense of balance and know that you're responding to the crit on an intellectual basis not on an emotional one. You're not rejecting good crits because you're feeling defensive and you're not accepting bad crits because you're intimidated. So it's very important to stay true to your copy. Make sense. Now next you promise the magic three word answer that can instantly end any copywriting argument and keep your strongest effort intact. You're having these discussions and let's say it's not a good, you don't like the solution the client has suggested or you don't think the problem the client thinks is a problem exists and you're not winning the argument. You're talking and she keeps saying or he keeps saying yeah but yeah but and they're not agreeing with you. The three words are let's test it. We used to do this at Weiss all the time. I've done it with all my clients as a matter of fact. Where somebody will come along and say well you know this headline is great but I'd like to see something like this instead. I'll say well let's test it. Or one client always loved to tell me what the market thinks. You know oh when people see this I'll think that and they'll just throw it away. You know well he didn't know what the market thought. He didn't have a clue. So I would just rather than argue with him and try to explain to him why the market didn't think that which is silly because I didn't know for sure what the market thought either. I just had a good gut on it. I'd say well hey why don't we test it. And more often than not both of them work quite well and when that happens when you have two headlines that work well then what happens is the customer or the client can bicycle the headlines. So in January he mails headline A and in February he mails headline B. And so then that means that your package doesn't wear out and you know tire is quickly. You don't have that attrition when the same people see the package for a second time because they're seeing a fresh headline a fresh lead maybe. So it ends up making you money. So let's test it as a really good way to solve major issues where you just can't come to a meeting of the client. Now moving to what some might call the dark side of in-comperting generally seminars and teleconferences like this and boot camps are positive about freelance writing but there are problems with it freelance conference just like there are problems with any other profession. So you next on our agenda is you are five times when you should demand your kill fee and kiss the client goodbye walk away. That sounds pretty radical. So when would you ever want to do that? I think I've only done it maybe once or twice in 33 years. So this is an infrequent episode. Yeah very. Well in terms of just outright firing the client absolutely. I've had a client that took two months to get back to me with the first set of crits and then they were way off the market. It was obvious that the person I was working with had no idea and I found out later this was their first time they'd ever worked with a copywriter and it was a larger company. So that surprised me. You know if it sat around and waited for clients to take that kind of time with every draft I'd write one package a year and the package would take year to get into the mail and I'd be making peanuts. So the client I need a feeling from the client that there is motivated to get this in the mail that I am and that means responding to crits and responding to researchers in a timely manner. What's timely? A day, a week. Well it depends on what the request is. If you're just asking for a copy review a week or so should be plenty. Sometimes these companies go into budget meetings and they go with administrative tasks for a period of time and so it may be a couple of weeks but certainly no more than that. They want your copy on deadline and presumably the reason is so they can get in the mail on deadline. If they're their own worst enemy when it comes turning around, you know crits on drafts that's a major red flag. What you wanted the copywriter, the ideal client, is somebody who will take your first draft and give you a few quick comments that will make it legal and maybe even strengthen the copy. Ram it through design a couple of weeks, test it within six weeks, you know, and roll out within three months. It's an ideal client because that means the royalties you're going to earn us will start coming in three months down the line or depending on how large your advance is, it's very quickly thereafter. When you have a client that drags their feet like that, you can't expect the royalties to come in for a year. So number one, you might fire a client when they drag their feet on the copy, they take too long to get back to you. What are some of the other situations? There are times when you'll find yourself doing, you'll be with a client who will be requesting these endless number of drafts and they just seem pointless. They just, well... I know who you're talking about. Yeah, I mean it just keeps going on and on and on. And I had a client and it was a very profitable client for me for many years and I went back to something I had written for these people to see what the last draft was on something that I'd written years earlier because they wanted to exert some of the copy. And I realized that I'd written 27 drafts. Now, you know, granted, they weren't complete drafts. They were just, you know, there might be a change in the product or a change in the premium or something like that. But you had to open the file and do work 27 times. 27 times. So that's no good. And quite often, you know, it wasn't because of my copy, it was because the editor kept changing his opinion of the economy or the premium kept changing or the product kept changing or new legal concerns came up or whatever. And so that kind of a client costs you a lot of money. And if they're making you a lot, that's fine. But if they're not, then they go to the bottom of the list. Okay. So we don't want clients that want 27 drafts? Mm-hmm. Another one is I have a friend, a copywriter friend, who wrote a really great health package and she gets it in the mail and it's not her headline. I've had that happen. She rewrote the headline. And so she called the client and she said, what the heck happened? You know, I had a kick butt headline. This thing's wimpy. You know, this package is in deep trouble. What happened? Oh, well, we got to the press and the editor changed his mind and liked the headline, so we had to change it. Another case with a different writer, it was a legal issue that came up at the last minute. What he called the right, the copy was changed. That client should be fired immediately. There's no way that you want to work for a client because then they're going to call back in a few weeks and they're going to say, hey, look, your package didn't work. And my friend said, what are you talking about? It wasn't my package. It was your package. You guys changed the copy. Exactly. So anyone that does that is just absolute poison. They have to respect the copywriter more than that. If somebody, if they're on the press at two o'clock in the morning and a lawyer says, you got to change this word and the headline to that word and the headline, I want a phone call. And then I can say, well, that's not going to work. Why don't we use this word instead? And we can hopefully come to some resolution that will save the package. So avoid clients who rewrite your copy without your permission or without telling you. What else will make it so bad that you would walk away from a client? Well, I touched on this one earlier. And it's basically the client who thinks you're taking dictation. Does that mean? Well, when they give you crits, they seem shocked that you don't just go through the copy and make the changes they've requested verbatim. If you can get around that with a careful care and feeding of that client, then great. But if they're insulted or angry that you won't just take dictation from them, then you've got a problem on your hands and that client is going to be less than productive for you. A lot of this we're talking about is dollar sense. So it's just common sense. But a lot of it too is the emotional side of it. As you're building your copyrighted career, you'll be doing more and more projects. And you want to love those projects. And you want to be excited about working on them and returning to them and keeping them fresh. And it's just no fun when you're dealing with people who are changing your copy or who are insulting to you or who don't value you and want you to just take dictation or whatever. And so there's an emotional side of this too. I have a friend. Returning to this particular friend today for some reason. But who really has a good balance on this. The money has to be good and I love the work. If the money isn't good but I love the work, I might hang around a little bit. Just because I love the work. But if I hate the work, no matter how good the money is, it's just not worth it. And then of course the final one is, the final time to move on is when you find a more lucrative client. When you find somebody that does any of the above things better, that gets back to you quicker on drafts, that collaborates better with you on the copy, that provides you with better research, that gives you the freedom that you need in order to produce winners. Someone who does all of those things is going to be a more lucrative client for you. And if your schedule is full or filling and you find somebody like that, then you're going to want to cut the clients at the bottom of the list that are less helpful in those ways. And so quite often through your career, you're going to have to make that choice. But I think it's important for copywriters to realize that we are business people first and to constantly be sorting that client list and saying, who's the best in terms of both income potential and joy of working for them? And who are the ones that, I kind of dread the phone call, I kind of dread working with. It's difficult. And also the ones that I tend not to do as well for. And constantly be sorting that list and constantly be looking for new people to replace the ones that are at the bottom. That makes sense. And we've got a few questions. First is from Joanne. Joanne says, where is the best source of finding upcoming marketing conferences where I can network with potential clients? Bob, maybe you'd be the help on that one. I draw a blank on that because as I said in the total package, I haven't been a conference animal. I'm not much of a network either, but let me give the answer and tell what I do. I actually don't like to network. So what I've done is to get myself out of these conferences, I actually don't attend to network. I will volunteer to give a presentation. That way you're viewed as the expert and all the attention that for that hour, that conference is focused on you. And you're someone who didn't just go there to go get business, which makes you look sort of needy, but you're someone who's there because you're a top person in your field and you're knowledgeable in email marketing or search engine optimization or whatever you're talking about. Now really the answer to where to go to conferences depends on what your market is. So for example, if you are interested in doing as Clayton does financial newsletters, well one place you could go is the Money Show. But if you're interested in, let's say, working for the type of clients that I do a lot of work for, business to business type companies, you would find an organization where the marketing managers of those companies go. It happens to be the Business Marketing Association, which is in Chicago. It's marketing www.marketing.org. If you want to do a lot of work in newsletters, financial, health newsletters, business newsletters, other publications, and newsletters as we've said is a great market for copywriters, then you want to get involved with an organization called NEPA, the Newsletter and Electron Publishers Association, and that's at www.newsletters.org. So those are some sources for you. I think another one too. I think you make an excellent point on going as a speaker. There are a couple of things there. We will be announcing our first conference here slated for next spring in the next couple of issues of the total package. That'll be an excellent opportunity to network because we'll have clients there as well as copywriters, marketing people as well as writers. Another one too. I've avoided this like the plague my entire life, but the DMA. I was just going to say the DMA, we overlooked at both national meetings that are held in New York or Washington as well as your local chapters, Direct Marketing Associations. Direct Marketing Associations for speakers. So if you would like to present on a particular aspect. And unless you hate the idea of public speaking, I think that's often a better way to go than just show up and be a networker. Yeah, and instead of being a teenage girl chasing rock stars through a lobby, you're an authority on a particular subject. Right, you're sort of the rock star for the hour. So we've answered where to find these marketing conferences to network with potential clients. Next question is from Mark, our friend Mark Kay. Does it make sense to try and get clients by doing spec assignments for companies that pay big and that you want to work for at the expense of a regular direct mail program for your services? In other words, when time is limited and your need of clients is one route better than the other. Should I just contact a few big companies and do an offer to work for them on spec? Or should I just do a mail blast and mail to a 500 marketing directors and hope someone's interested? Well, I always did all of the above. My approach was to keep a lot of lines in the water. We're obviously talking about somebody at the beginning of a career here who doesn't already have a track record in the history with these clients. Six steps that I took early in my career to generate customers, to generate clients from a fairly large list of big mailers that I was going after. And I did that and that yield clients that I can trace every dollar I make to today. And in addition to that, I also took the spec route. I also went to, I called it being a direct mail bully. I went to companies whose controls I felt I could beat. And I did one of several things. Either I offered them, I called them on the phone and offered them a deal where I would write for free and return for royalty if I beat their control, or a bonus if they won't pay royalties. And then I also would just go away if I had time on my hands and felt inspired like I could really crank this out in a day or two or in a relatively short period of time. I'd just sit down and I'd write a kick butt new package for the company and send it to them and then say, you know, let me know what you think. I want to do this for you and I'd be more than happy to finish it up and then say what they see then chances are you've got a deal there. I would then negotiate either some advance or some fee for finishing the package, but now you're in the door. I think that answer makes sense. And I don't think of these markers as either or. People will say to me, well, should I write articles or should I send out mailings? And I say, why is it either or? You should write articles and you should send out mailings until you're established and have until you're at the point where more people are coming to you than you can handle. When you're not at that point, then you have the time to do multiple self promotion activities and you should. I've had several letters come into me too that I'd like to address real quickly. If we can, we seem to have a few minutes left. We do. One of the emails that I get over and over again is, it's like it's from people who are looking for excuses why they can't succeed in this business. And the excuse is never with a copy they have to do with barriers to entry. I got a letter the other day from a woman who wanted to know is there sexual discrimination in direct marketing? I think I have the same one. This is mine's from Paris. Well, I wouldn't. We're only giving first names. I'd take it. All right. I've had several from people in Europe wanting to know if there's a barrier to non-Americans writing for the American market. I get that a lot. I'd like to hear how you answer that. I've had people ask me if there's racial discrimination going on. Let's take them one at a time. Sexual discrimination. Absolutely not. Not even a shred of it have I encountered or seen in this marketplace. For one thing, most, and of course we're talking about men not wanting to hire women here. In the first place, most of the people who do the hiring and most of the marketing managers are women. So, you know, all my bosses are women almost. I think I have maybe one or two clients who I'm working with, a male marketing director. The rest of them are all women. So, I think it would be kind of silly for them to discriminate against their own just on the basis of sex. And I have friends who are, happen to be females who are doing extremely well in this market. You all have read about Carleen Anglite Cole and you've all probably heard me talk about Kim Krause. These are two wonderful writers who create kick butt controls for their clients. They're well known as A-level writers and they make a fortune. So, if you're looking for an excuse, sexual discrimination will not be one of them. It doesn't hold water. On the racial discrimination thing, the same thing holds. There's no reason why a person of any ethnic background, why response or money or customers generated by a person of any ethnic background would be less valuable to a client than people in money that are generated by another writer. So, again, these people's whole lives turn on finding great writers. People can produce results. You produce results and it doesn't matter really who you are. As far as the country discrimination is concerned, we're obviously talking about people from Great Britain and Australia and Canada and other countries that are English speaking wanting to know are there barriers for them to entry and the only barrier there really, there is something to consider there. It's not really a barrier. And that's just when the sales copy rewrite is colloquial in nature. In other words, we're not following all the great rules of grammar and so forth. We're using idioms and we're using metaphors and similes that our prospects are familiar with. And those figures of speech are quite often not familiar to people from other countries or as familiar as they are to us. And so sometimes when you're working with writers from other countries, there'll be minor issues crop up in the copy. You know, the British are famous for calling the trunk of a car the boot. Just things like that. They can be easily handled. I would encourage people from other countries to immerse themselves in American culture as much as possible, get a handle for the differences in our languages. But having said that, people are the same everywhere and the things that motivate us are the same. And so I encourage people from other countries to take a whack at the American markets. In fact, I have a man from the Netherlands right now writing for me and another gentleman from Canada writing for me. And I'm very happy so far with the work they've done for me. So it's overcomable. What about is there age discrimination? I get often emails from people who say, I wanted to have a second career. I'm interested in copywriting, but I'm 60. I'm 65. 70. I don't think so. I was surprised and lighted at the AWAI conference at the number of people who are making copywriting a second career. And I think it's wonderful. I think it's great that that's happening. I can see that when a marketing manager is hiring a young person who's just starting up, they don't have the impression that this person has moved on from something else. They're kind of like Vash. When you go to hire a 50-year-old that has no experience or very little experience in the marketplace, there might be some reservation at first. But remember, the copy that you're writing, the samples that you're showing, that speaks volumes, that's what it's all about. And it's really not about your age or whether this is your second or third or fourth career or your first. It's about the power of the words that you put on the page. If those words are powerful, this is all about self-interest for the clients. If you can sell their product, they want you. That's the bottom line. If you can sell their product as well as or better than they are now, then they're going to line up to get on your dance card. That's all there is to it. Terrific. Do you have other questions there in front of you? Yeah, I just wanted to... There was a question that I received about, are there any other ways to get compensation? I just wanted to share one that I tried once and it worked out quite well for me. A particular client wanted me to work with him, but for one reason or another, the upfront arrangement that I was used to just didn't work that well for him. What I did was I structured a deal where I could work for less on the front end, certainly enough to cover monthly nut, but I took stock options in his company because I was absolutely convinced that I could quadruple the size of that company. I went and I said, okay, look, I need this much to live every month and to live well, but that's not as... That's basically all you can afford right now. If I didn't come to work for you full-time or exclusively with you, I would be making this much many, many times more. If I'm going to come and make this investment in your company, I want an option that says X years down the road, three years, whatever you think it'll take you to really produce the growth. Three years down the road, I want stock options worth X percent of your company. Today those stock options are worthless, but I'm just going to... The money that I'll get when I cash those options in will compensate me for this time that I'm spending with you. He went for it. It was a nice, very, very healthy payday for me when I cashed those options out. Be creative about these deals. You may find somebody would only use that kind of approach with a client that I was absolutely convinced was set up for explosive growth and the missing ingredient was me. If I find a client like that, then I'm more than willing to take that kind of a deal. Terrific. On that note, we are at the end of our hour and a half for today. Thank you, Clayton. We want to thank you once again. Next week's call is number three in the series titled, Write Better Coffee, Faster, Produce up to twice as many projects per year and get bigger winners more often, which is something we all want to learn. That is to dial into Wednesday at noon like they always are. Great. Thanks a lot, guys. Thanks, everybody. Bye-bye. Bye-bye. Bye-bye. Thanks, everybody. Bye-bye. Bye-bye. Bye-bye. Bye-bye. Bye-bye.
